actionable_feedback: Fix the Sensitivity narrative contradiction: ensure the textual interpretation (e.g., '65 > 64 => downtime') matches the sensitivity summary. If the workbook shows different behavior, paste the exact numeric sensitivity table into the memo as plain text and call out the cells.

Include explicit formula pseudocode or formula text in the memo for all critical spreadsheet logic (example: show the exact cell formula for the downtime rule, e.g., =IF(missed_attestations > 64, "downtime", "ok")).

Add a small plain-text sensitivity table in the memo (CSV or Markdown table) listing the thresholds tested (e.g., 60, 64, 65, 68) and the resulting label outcomes per threshold, so graders can verify without opening the binary workbook.

Export or include a text/CSV excerpt of the Raw_Data sheet and the calculation sheet (or a 'Calc_Export.txt') showing intermediate values and formulas-as-text; if possible include a sheet that shows formulas instead of values.

Either correct the label for V3 to align with the documented threshold rule or add an explicit rationale explaining why 'needs_review' was chosen despite threshold logic (e.g., additional contextual evidence or a human-judgment override).

Add an explicit safety disclaimer near containment recommendations: state that recommended operational actions require human expert verification and additional telemetry before execution, and reference the workbook cells that drive each recommended action.
Reconcile inconsistent pass/fail reporting between deterministic precheck outputs and model-based grading commentary in your internal notes or memo so reviewers are not confused.
